claims.text: Large language models struggle to catch errors in their own outputs when the review happens in the same session that produced them.
claims.source_quote: Large language models struggle to catch errors in their own outputs when the review happens in the same session that produced them.

claims.text: Cross-Context Review (CCR) is a method where the review is conducted in a fresh session with no access to the production conversation history.
claims.source_quote: This paper introduces Cross-Context Review (CCR), a straightforward method where the review is conducted in a fresh session with no access to the production conversation history.

claims.text: The controlled experiment used 30 artifacts including code, technical documents, and presentation scripts.
claims.source_quote: We ran a controlled experiment: 30 artifacts (code, technical documents, presentation scripts) with 150 injected errors

claims.text: The controlled experiment included 150 injected errors across the 30 artifacts.
claims.source_quote: 30 artifacts (code, technical documents, presentation scripts) with 150 injected errors

claims.text: The experiment tested four review conditions: same-session Self-Review (SR), repeated Self-Review (SR2), context-aware Subagent Review (SA), and Cross-Context Review (CCR).
claims.source_quote: tested under four review conditions -- same-session Self-Review (SR), repeated Self-Review (SR2), context-aware Subagent Review (SA), and Cross-Context Review (CCR)

claims.text: The experiment conducted a total of 360 reviews.
CCR achieved an F1 score of 28.6%.
SR (same-session Self-Review) achieved an F1 score of 24.6%.
CCR outperformed SR with a statistically significant p-value of 0.008.
The effect size (Cohen's d) for CCR vs SR was 0.52.
SR2 (repeated Self-Review) achieved an F1 score of 21.7%.
CCR outperformed SR2 with a statistically significant p-value of less than 0.001.
The effect size (Cohen's d) for CCR vs SR2 was 0.72.
SA (context-aware Subagent Review) achieved an F1 score of 23.8%.
CCR outperformed SA with a statistically significant p-value of 0.004.
The effect size (Cohen's d) for CCR vs SA was 0.57.
Reviewing twice in the same session (SR2) did not significantly outperform reviewing once (SR), with p=0.11.
claims.source_quote: reviewing twice in the same session did not beat reviewing once (p=0.11)

claims.text: Repetition alone does not explain CCR's performance advantage over SR.
claims.source_quote: which rules out repetition as an explanation for CCR's advantage

claims.text: The benefit of CCR comes from context separation itself.
claims.source_quote: The benefit comes from context separation itself.

claims.text: CCR works with any model.
claims.source_quote: CCR works with any model, needs no infrastructure, and costs only one extra session.

claims.text: CCR requires no additional infrastructure beyond a standard LLM session.
claims.source_quote: CCR works with any model, needs no infrastructure, and costs only one extra session.

claims.text: CCR costs only one extra session compared to standard single-session review.
